title: The Morning Routine
completeDialogue: You found it! Fantastic work, Bill. You already know how to use strings, functions, and dot notation! You are going to be a natural JavaScript Developer. Come on, let's head over to the Bakery to catch a thief!

phases.dialogue: Welcome to the agency, Rookie! I'm Chief Inspector Bunny. Don't let the long ears fool you—I've cracked cases from here to the Carrot District. Before we can dive into the backlogs, we need to make sure your Detective Terminal can interface with the environment. In JavaScript, we make the system talk using console.log. Give it a spin by logging a simple hello string!
phases.lesson: console.log("...") is the most fundamental JavaScript command. It prints text to the terminal.
notes: Use console.log("text"); to talk to the computer.

active_hotspots.hover_text: Your detective terminal interface. It's uncalibrated.

phases.dialogue: Perfect, our terminal is synced up! Now, I have a bit of an embarrassing problem… I lost my main investigation journal somewhere in this messy office. Let's use the terminal to run a radar sweep of the environment. To use an agency command tool, you type its name, followed by your targeted destination enclosed in string quotes.
phases.output:
Room scanned.
→ 3 interactive zones detected:
   • desk
   • plant
   • drawers
phases.lesson: A function is a named tool. scan("office") passes a string argument to tell the function what to scan.
notes: HINT: Use inspect("target"); to look closer.

active_hotspots.hover_text: Ready for an environmental room scan sweep.

phases.dialogue: Great work! The terminal radar detected my primary desk, a decorative potted plant, and some wooden filing drawers. I almost always leave my journal right on my workspace. Let's redirect our inspection tool to scope out the desk area.
phases.output:
Inspecting desk...
→ Desk contains:
   • coffee_mug
   • open_book
   • clipboard
phases.lesson: Same function, different argument. The string you pass changes what the function targets.
notes: Objects have actions — connect with a dot: object.action();

phases.dialogue: Hmm, no journal in plain sight. But look! The scanner found a coffee_mug, an open_book, and an agency clipboard. In code, items are treated as 'objects', and we can make them perform specific actions by connecting them with a dot. Let's execute a read action on that book to see if it's my journal.
phases.output:
Reading open_book...
→ Webster's English Dictionary.
→ Nothing useful here.
phases.lesson: Pattern: object.method("argument"); Code can run successfully and still not solve the puzzle — that's exploration!

active_hotspots.hover_text: An opened dictionary is propped up against the lamp.

phases.dialogue: Ah, just my old dictionary. False alarm! But don't get discouraged, Rookie—investigating means crossing off wrong leads! Let's pivot and read the clipboard instead. Don't forget to use that dot notation structure to point to it.
phases.output:
Reading clipboard...
→ Note: 'Keep journal locked in drawers.
         Hide key in foliage.'
notes: CLUE: Journal is locked in the drawers.
CLUE: Key is hidden in the foliage (plant).

active_hotspots.hover_text: An agency clipboard holding maintenance logs.

phases.dialogue: Aha! The note says I locked my journal in the drawers and hid the key inside the 'foliage'... that has to mean the potted plant over in the corner! Run your standard inspect function again, but adjust your target argument to search the plant.
phases.output:
Scanning plant...
→ Glint of metal detected!
→ A silver key is visible among the leaves.
phases.lesson: You used a function you already know on a new target based on a discovered clue.
notes: VISUAL: Silver key spotted in the plant leaves!

active_hotspots.hover_text: A decorative potted plant sitting in the light.

phases.dialogue: Boom! There it is—a silver key! Now, in this terminal system, *you* are an object too, and your system identity is self. To pick up evidence and add it to your active inventory, you need to command yourself to take it.
phases.output:
[Item Acquired: Silver Key] 🗝
→ Key added to inventory.
phases.lesson: self refers to you — the player object. self.take("key") invokes your pick up sequence.
notes: NOTE: You have the key. Now target the drawers.

active_hotspots.hover_text: The key is glittering within the branches.

phases.dialogue: Key acquired! Now, the clipboard note explicitly verified that the journal is stashed away inside the filing drawers. Let's move our focus over there and execute an open command. Some actions don't require any inputs inside the parentheses!
phases.output:
ERROR: The drawers are locked. Access denied.
→ ScopeError: container.isLocked === true
→ You must unlock the drawers before opening them!
phases.lesson: Code runs in exact order. An ERROR is just the system telling you what it needs next.

phases.dialogue: Whoops! Did you catch that system Error text? Code works on pure, unyielding logic—we can't pull open a locked drawer container without unlocking it first! Let's pass our silver key string into the drawers' unlock action method.
phases.output:
Click.
→ Security lock disengaged. 🔓
→ Padlock status: OPEN.
phases.lesson: You passed an item name string into a method as an execution argument.
notes: Lock disabled. Now safe to open.

active_hotspots.hover_text: The locking mechanism requires key authentication.

phases.dialogue: Beautiful! There's that satisfying click. The security latch is completely disengaged. Now that the lock condition evaluates to false, run that exact same open command from earlier with the empty parentheses!
phases.output:
Drawers opening...
→ [Chief Inspector Bunny's Journal Acquired] 📓

★  LEVEL COMPLETE — The Morning Routine  ★
phases.lesson: Some methods require no arguments — just empty execution blocks: drawers.open().

active_hotspots.hover_text: Unlocked State. Safe to pull open.
